title: Spin-Orbit Torque and Magnetization Switching in 2D Ferromagnetic Devices
authors: Bao-Huei Huang, Hong Guo, Yu-Hui Tang
abstract: Current-induced spin-orbit torque has emerged as a powerful technique for manipulating magnetization switching of ferromagnet/nonmagnet (FM/NM) based memory cell. By investigating nonequilibrium spin torque effect in a van der Waals heterobilayer, trigonal $\text{Cr}_{3}\text{Te}_{4}/\text{PtTe}_{2}$, the first-principles quantum transport calculations are applied to determine both local spin induction, resulting from Rashba-Edelstein effect in the FM layer, and spin current injection, flowing from the NM to the FM layer. Our work reveals that local spin induction significantly generates the fieldlike torque, which primarily governs the switching current in systems with strong in-plane magnetic anisotropy. Our work emphasizes the importance of optimizing spin Hall effect in the NM layer for perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA)-based magnetization switching and maximizing the Rashba effect in the FM layer for in-plane magnetic anisotropy (IMA)-based switching.
summary: This work theoretically analyzes how spin-orbit torque drives magnetization switching in 2D ferromagnetic heterostructures. By decomposing the total torque into fieldlike and dampinglike components, it reveals that different physical mechanisms—local spin induction versus spin current injection—dominate switching depending on whether the material favors in-plane or perpendicular magnetism. This guides optimization for energy-efficient spintronic memory design.
main_problem: The study investigates the mechanisms governing current-induced spin-orbit torque (SOT) for magnetization switching in 2D ferromagnetic devices.
main_result: The paper distinguishes between Fieldlike Torque (FLT), dominated by local spin induction (Rashba-Edelstein effect in FM layers), and Dampinglike Torque (DLT), associated with spin current injection, showing their distinct roles depending on the magnetic anisotropy regime (IMA vs. PMA).
method: The research employs first-principles quantum transport calculations using DFT/NEGF methods combined with macrospin simulations solving the LLG equation.
model_or_system: The model uses a $	ext{Cr}_3	ext{Te}_4/	ext{PtTe}_2$ van der Waals heterobilayer, analyzing spin dynamics governed by terms including spin current injection ($\Phi$) and local spin induction ($	ext{TSO}_{	ext{FM}}$).
key_observables: Spin current injection ($\Phi$), local spin induction ($	ext{TSO}_{	ext{FM}}$), Fieldlike Torque ($T_{	ext{FL}}$), Dampinglike Torque ($T_{	ext{DL}}$), and the critical switching current density ($J_{sw}$).
important_parameters: Strong in-plane magnetic anisotropy (IMA) vs. perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA); torque efficiencies ($\eta$); damping parameter ($\alpha$).
main_assumptions: Calculations are performed assuming a clean limit and often utilize approximations like the small angle approximation for magnetization dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spin textures, band structures showing Rashba splitting due to SOC, and $M-J_c$ loops demonstrating switching behavior under different anisotropy conditions.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the theoretical framework of CISOT components (FLT vs. DLT), derives the necessary equations based on material properties (e.g., SHE/REE effects), and applies these models to predict switching currents for specific magnetic anisotropy regimes in the vdW heterostructure.
why_interesting: The detailed quantum mechanical decomposition of torques into local induction versus injected current provides a highly granular understanding of spin-charge conversion, which is crucial for designing next-generation spintronic devices that interact with quantum phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

For nonequilibrium calculations, the charge current is applied along the 𝑦direction.

FIG. 1. (a) Spin Hall effect and (b) Rashba-Edelstein effect in the NM layer induce the spin current injection from the NM to the FM layer, which is known as spin current injection. (c) Within the FM layer, Rashba-Edelstein effect leads to local spin induction. (d) Both the spin current injection and local spin induction contribute to a current-induced spin density 𝝈∥x when a charge current density Jc ∥y is applied, initiating CISOT acting on the FM magnetization in the fieldlike (𝑇FL) and dampinglike (𝑇DL) directions. Fig. 3, the band structure exhibits inversion symmetry along 𝑘𝑥for arbitrary orientations of M. This symmetry implies that an 𝑥-directed current can generate an additional 𝑧-polarized spin accumulation. In such cases, the system would generate two distinct current-induced spin-orbit fields, one along ˆy and another along ˆz, both contributing to the CISOT depending on the current direction. In this work, we restrict our analy- sis to the simpler geometry where the current flows along 𝑦, corresponding to the conventional Rashba-Edelstein scenario.
